sourceMovie.title: Seven Samurai
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Seven Samurai (1954) as a action theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The action and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: 47 Ronin
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Seven Samurai clicked for you, 47 Ronin (2013) is a strong next stop: both trade in action and drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: The Karate Kid Part II
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Seven Samurai clicked for you, The Karate Kid Part II (1986) is a strong next stop: both trade in action and drama, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Seven Samurai?
faq.answer: Try 47 Ronin, The Karate Kid Part II, Black Rain—each shares DNA with Seven Samurai while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Seven Samurai?
faq.answer: Seven Samurai (1954) is best described as Action, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Seven Samurai worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.5/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this action lane.
